![][image1]**Short Guide to Writing ** 

**Department of  Mathematics and  Informatics**

**the End-of-Studies Project (ESP) Report** 

**(2023)** 

Abstract  

The ESP and its writing process hold paramount importance in students’ academic journeys. To that end, this  short guide aims to facilitate the production and meeting the requirements of a good project report. It  provides advice on how to put together prior knowledge, ideas, skills (hard and soft) and internship  experience into a suitably organised written report. It briefly tells the expected contents of the introduction,  body chapters and conclusion. Each of these components has a specific function, which this guide attempts  to illustrate. Moreover, the guide explains the role of obligatory and optional materials before and after the  substance of the report. These range from acknowledgements and the table of contents in the beginning to  appendices in the very end. The guide also suggests a citation style for referencing. Together with the  template and citation style manual, this guide ultimately purports to spare students some organisational  effort to focus more on creativity and efficiency in project concretisation and report writing. 

**1.Introduction** 

Writing the end-of-studies (ESP) report is not simply a mechanical or superficial task.  Rather, it is a process that requires, and equally reflects, long-term planning of the end-product.  These guidelines are not meant to function as an exclusive reference for the ESP composition,  but aim, instead, at helping students conceptualise, structure and better present their reports. 

**2\. The threshold** 

For the report to be clear, and for its writer to be well-understood, several basic, yet crucial,  criteria must obtain. The first is language requirements. The writer is highly expected to be too  meticulous in their writing style. The report must be free from spelling and grammar mistakes.  Embellishing the style with suitable, or even creative, word choice and varied sentence structure  adds flavour to the overall report interface. Second is that the report should be well-presented,  self-explanatory and all-inclusive. It features the typical components of a technical report whose readers expect to see a developed body preceded and followed by supplementary materials. The    
1   
rest of these guidelines explain that structure. 

**3\. Report structure** 

**3.1 Introductory supplements** 

In the report, early pages introduce, explain and support the main text that consists of an  introduction, body and conclusion. They are given Roman numerals (i, ii, iii, iv, etc), while the  first page in the introduction is given number 1\. 

**Table 1 :** *Report components (in order)* 

| Element  | ESP |
| :---- | :---- |
| Title page  | Obligatory |
| Dedications  | Optional |
| Acknowledgements  | Obligatory |
| List of acronymes, abbreviations, glossary, etc.  | If necessary |
| Abstract  | Obligatory |
| Table of contents  | Obligatory |
| List of figures  | If necessary |
| List of tables  | If necessary |
| Introduction (page numbers start here)  | Obligatory |
| General conclusion  | Obligatory |
| List of references  | Obligatory |
| Appendices  | If necessary |

**3.1.1 Title page (also called cover page)**  

As the first page in the report, the title page mentions the project title, student(s) name(s),  date, names and logos of the training institution (ENSETM) and internship enterprise, names of  tutors or supervisors, etc. The dispatched **template** includes a title page. 

**3.1.2 Acknowledgements** 

Placed immediately after the dedications. The final version of any report reflects a whole  process whereby writers receive different kinds of help, support, mentoring and guidance. To show  gratitude and indebtedness, they recognise the active role of institutions and, especially, individuals  in the concretisation of the report. Students acknowledge such support as academic supervision,  internship supervision, access to documents, resources or facilities, training on software, invitations  to workshops, help with securing internship, or proof-reading, to name a few.

2   
**3.1.3 Abstract** 

The abstract is a short, self-explanatory paragraph put in the beginning of the report. Its word count  ranges between 150-200 (See example above). It gives readers enough information to help them get  the gist of the report and fathom its subject. It equally helps readers decide whether to read the whole  report or not, depending on their interests. The abstract never mentions something that does not  occur in the report. That’s why it is advisable to write it last. 

The abstract consists of the following elements, in brief: 

∙ Project discipline, sub-discipline, domain or general theory 

∙ Problem description & project objective 

∙ Presentation of the utilised method(s) 

∙ Key finding(s), principal solution(s) or main conclusion 

∙ Key recommendation 

It is equally advisable to include five keywords right after the abstract. 

**3.1.4 Table of contents** 

Occurs after the abstract. It provides a detailed view on the report chapters and sections along with  their associated pages. 

**3.1.5 List of tables and list of figures** 

They follow the abstract, with a separate page devoted to each. Figures can be maps, schemas,  visuals, pie charts, wordles, demos, diagrams, etc. Including tables and figures facilitates illustrating ideas and communicating concepts or practices. Tables and figures are labelled and captioned. For  instance, the second figure in chapter 3 would be captioned as:  

Figure 3.2: Schema of data flow. 

**3.1.6 Lists of symbols, abbreviations and/or acronyms** 

These are devoted a separate page each, when necessary to add. They serve to put forward any special symbols, abbreviations or acronyms that the report uses frequently, but may not be  understood unless explained by the writer. 

**3.2 Introduction** 

Composed of paragraphs, the introduction generally consists of 2 to 3 pages that deal  successively with three elements. The first is context. This point situates the report within its broader  location to give readers a clear introductory idea and familiarise them with the subject under study. The second is the ‘tackled point’. This refers to delimiting the subject by raising the problem,  explaining the rationale behind its choice, as well as the project scope and objectives. This may also 

3   
include a research question. Project results or internship findings are not included in the introduction. The third point in the introduction is ‘report constituents’. This announces the main parts, chapters  and sections of the report. The chapters that follow the introduction are described briefly. 

Usually, report writing does not start with the introduction. It would be better to write it, or finalise  it, after the general structure of the report body takes a clear shape. It serves to introduce the  subject area, spotlight the problem, pinpoint the rationale behind choosing it, highlight the report’s  scope, announce the key objectives and put forward the report plan, or key components, sub-parts,  chapters, etc. When the introduction respects these tips, the reader is prepared more to have a 

glimpse of the report as a whole and can follow its logical development smoothly. **3.3 Body of the graduation report**   
This is the main, and biggest, part of the document. It consists of several chapters, whereby  the report substance is displayed. **Its page number ranges between 60 and 80**, not more. Extra  documents, lists, procedures, user manuals, guides, etc, can be put in the appendices.  

The report body consists, generally, of three to four chapters, balanced evenly in size. For  instance, if a report deals with installing a new system within a given enterprise, its body can be  organised as follows: 

*Chapter1: Background and Specification* 

This chapter aims at locating the project in its broad organisational, practical, scientific and  academic context. Consequently, it consists of three key components, namely: 

∙ Presentation of the hosting enterprise. Included in this necessary information about the  enterprise, especially pertinent demographic data, field of intervention, activities and  likely stakeholders that relate to the graduation project. This helps explain why the project  is addressing the problem it works out. 

∙ Problem description: This point describes and explains clearly and adequately the problem  that has been identified for your project to solve.  

∙ Review of the literature: Here you review previous studies, methods or tools that pertain to  your solution. As you are not expected to start from scratch, this point covers any previous  studies, theories, theoretical frameworks, documents, products or solutions whose  ‘deficiencies’ your project aims to transcend or fix. If your project aims to replace or  extend some technology, describe it here and explain its lacunas. 

N.B: Avoid copying and pasting very long sentences or long paragraphs, as this is interpreted as  plagiarism. When quoting from a reference, abide by the ‘kiss’ principle (Keep it short and simple),  and take only information relevant to the development of your project or solution. Consult relevant  sources and refer to them properly in text and in the list of references. Your ESP report examiners  usually verify your references. As such, it would add more value to your report to consult solid  sources (books, studies, research papers, journal articles, government documents, annual reports, 

4   
conference proceedings, etc), rather than web pages, especially slightly controlled ones such as  Wikipedia, blogs or digital portfolios. 

*Chapter 2: Design and Implementation* 

The ultimate goal of this chapter is to give your reader a clear idea about the system (technology,  product, solution, etc) that you have created to get your results or findings. Analysis at this level builds  a clear bridge between the theoretical framework and the solution you come up with. This helps the  reader understand your choices, decisions, solution impact and the logic behind your conclusions.  Your process or methodological design is described in text. Mainly, the series of steps is explained  in chronological order. The **ways in which** certain techniques, software or applications are utilised  to fix the given problem are described too. You can depend on diagrams, such as UML diagrams, to  demonstrate your processes and procedures. You have an opportunity to show your familiarity with  concepts and mastery of the tools by justifying your choices. While implementing your solution, if you  faced some problems that you managed to fix, add them to the description of the process. 

*Chapter 3: Results and & Evaluation* 

This chapter is devoted to discussing the extent to which the project has achieved its goals.  To do that, some questions can be considered, such as: Has the suggested solution been tried  actually? If yes, what impact has it had? Or, has the given solution been tested? If yes, depending  on what test? What were the results of the testing process? Did they highlight strengths or  weaknesses? Otherwise, if you think that certain tests will further show the feasibility, practicality  or importance of your design, describe them in this chapter. You can also compare your project to  other successful ones, if any, especially in terms of good aspects and practices. This chapter  equally allows critically evaluating your process as a whole. For example, if you faced some  problems that you could not fix, for some reason or another, add them here. Anyway, this chapter  can serve well to demonstrate confidence in the chosen process, no matter whether it has yielded  the intended outcome completely or partially. If there are weaknesses or lacunas, you can describe  them, but do not suggest ideas to fix them, as this should go to the next chapter. 

*Chapter 4: Recommendations & Reflection* 

The graduation project is a human endeavour. As such, you may not have achieved the level,  quality, depth or efficiency as you planned in the first place. Also, it is highly expected that, while  working on your project, you recognised new pathways, modifications or possibilities that you  could not tackle because you run short of time, space or resources. Whichever ideas you believe  can be a starting point for future work, add as recommendations. Whichever changes you considered   
5   
necessary but could not execute, suggest as recommendations to future students to start from. The  second part of this chapter is devoted to reflection on the main takeaways from the internship and  graduation project as a whole. What skills did you put to the test, whether hard or soft? What lessons  have you drawn for life? For instance, did the experience push you to change any assumptions,  concepts or ideas you used to take for granted? In what ways you found the graduation internship  complimentary with your ENSET journey? Possibly you faced some challenges while working on  your project, how differently will you deal with them if you face them again in the future? No  matter, this chapter is meant to enhance your life-long learning skills. 

N.B. Each chapter starts with a specific introduction and ends with a specific conclusion. This way,  the reader sees the logical development and sequencing of the different chapters and parts. 

**3.4 General conclusion** 

Generally, conclusions serve for a slew of purposes. Three key ones are expected. First, the  conclusion reiterates and reminds readers of the objectives that the introduction delimits. Also, the  report structure can be restated, while the writer can put **in different words** the project synthesis,  rationale and/or main objectives. Key findings can be stressed briefly too. Then, the current situation 

or the fixed problem can be evaluated briefly. A critical analysis of the findings or outcome can  occur here as well. Second, key implications and limitations of the project are highlighted.  Implications relate to key findings of what has been done, while in limitations we admit and  recognise the obstacles that hampered achieving all the objectives as planned in the first place. This  is solidified by reflecting on learning. You can think about questions such as: What are your key  take-aways from writing this report? What core/soft skills did you activate while working on it,  including during the internship? Which core/soft skills you have discovered that you need to work  more on? How do you think you will benefit from this whole learning journey in the future? Etc.  Third, the conclusion can open the project to new perspectives, or recommendations for future  engineers to work on. The aim is to, again, situate the project within its broader discipline and pave  the way for future researchers and engineers. With these components, the conclusion brings the  whole report to a logical and satisfactory ending. 

**3.5 List of references** 

All activities of research and development require consulting references for theories, methods,  definitions, previous findings, etc. Mentioning references in your project argumentation backs up its  scientific merit. References equally enable readers to locate every reference or work cited in the  report (books, journal articles, government reports, scientific publications, Youtube lectures,  university tutorials, etc.). They can also guide future readers and researchers to deepen their  knowledge about the given discipline, topic, solution, etc. In principle, however, references show   
6   
self-respect and recognition of scientific ethics. We mention other people’s work in order to  highlight the originality of ours. 

The list of references occurs at the end of the report, right after the conclusion. We list **only** the  sources mentioned in the report. That is, when writing the report, we quote sources, and then make  sure they occur in the list. Though there are many citation styles, the one used mostly in computer  science and informatics engineering is the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers style 

(IEEE). To use it, put numbers \[1\], \[2\], etc in-text. Then, references occur in the same order in the  list, based on the Chicago citation style. A **citation guide** is dispatched with the **template**. 

**3.6 Appendices** 

In this part, we put supplementary information, documents, lists, procedures, etc. They are  part of the overall report, but either its argumentation does not depend on them heavily, or they are very long for including in the **60 to 80 pages** of the report body. Appendices are labelled with a  capital alphabet and a title. For example, we put: Appendix A: List of ……, Appendix B: Letter  from ……., Appendix C, etc.). Appendices are optional. When you decide to put them, give them  Roman numbers, similar to the pages before the introduction. 

**4\. ESP actual presentation** 

**A template** is dispatched to describe and precise the ordering and composition of the ESP and  internship (See **Template**).

7
